text: Hi Mariano, in similar examples I’ve always felt it’s hard to follow which inputs produce which outputs. Is there a way to pause the tracing and maybe manually step through the examples? Have you felt the need for it?

One thing I can't stop thinking about is Explorable Explanations. Easy ones are just diagrams that allow moving sliders / points in a graph to change some part of the output. While creating static diagrams is pretty straight-forward in tools like excel, adding just a bit more interactivity requires libraries like d3 and hours/days of work. I have a concrete example of such an explanation almost finished and I'll post it as soon a I finish it, probably tomorrow.

There are a few languages for explorables, such as tangle and idyll. ObservableHQ makes things a bit easier in JS/d3. <@UCL9Q87P0> had a project along these lines at some point but I forget the details...

The explorables community has a slack as well (run by Nicky Case) so is be curious there thoughts on tooling/language. I think Nicky had a project in that space too but I forget offhand

I fear it’s the sort of problem that seems easy until you start to dig in

Few years back, I tried to make an well polished Bayes Rule explorable <https://gist.github.com/wtaysom/6f7ee490ec7642de6217>.  (Just download the ZIP and open bayes.html in Chrome.)  Classic 90% effort for the last 10% of the niceness.

Few years back, I tried to make an well polished Bayes Rule explorable <https://gist.github.com/wtaysom/6f7ee490ec7642de6217>.  (Just download the ZIP and open bayes.html in Chrome.)  Classic 90% effort for the last 10% of the niceness.

<@UAL7940NM> I like your question a lot. Don’t really have a good answer for it, but wanted to mention that my impression is that we all tend to try to build a big solution for better programming. That often leads to us being interested in what others do on the surface, but it rarely happens that our visions align enough so that we start collaborating (I tried to make that a little more explicit with my earlier effort to find shared principles for this group).

I wish it would be easier for us to extract smaller problems that we all have to solve on the way to a holistic solution, and maybe increase the chance of collaboration a little. I feel like we have built an awesome community of very capable individuals here that could pull off amazing things, if we can find ways to make us more than just the sum of the parts.

We should still all keep working on individual projects — diversity of ideas is very important in a space where we are mostly exploring. However, identifying the bits where more of us struggle with a concrete albeit smaller problem could tap into our collective power to solve it quicker or better or both.

Smaller artifacts like an explorable explanation of a concept more of us need to solve for their own projects could be a great way to collaborate for a defined amount of time and get some sense of achievement at the same time, which I’m sure will also have positive impact on our individual projects.

Of course, a first step in that direction would be to better understand what each of us are working on currently, and what some of the (smaller) research questions are we currently think about and maybe also make that information a little easier to find.

root.text: When I think about ways to "make programming easier", I often find myself thinking about the same small set of end-user problems that I'd like to be easier to solve. I'd love to hear whether you also have scenarios like these in your head that you use to evaluate your ideas.

Feedback is highly appreciated :wink:

Cool! Some random notes:

• I didn't get a good sense of the *purpose* of this algorithm at the start - what problem it's supposed to solve. (I guess it's edge detection...?) Don't be afraid of more words :slightly_smiling_face:
• I also would appreciate some intuition/analogy in words at the start of how/why the algorithm works. I love this method of organizing explanations <https://betterexplained.com/articles/adept-method/>
• I would consider putting the your first interactive visualization at the end as opposed to the beginning of the article. If the point of the algorithm is edge detection, it's hard to see that in placing single points. Maybe that interactive has more to do with *how* the algorithm works, while the interactive at the end shows the result of the algorithm.
• It would be neat in the last interactive viz if you could moreover the graph and it would show you the point in the diagram. Even better if it as was connected to the original picture!

Hi Steve, thanks for the detailed feedback! I did indeed focus on the interactive diagrams and only spent half an hour or so on the text. You're right that there's barely any description of how the hough transform works or why it's useful/necessary. I should definitely improve on that. Changing the order of the diagrams is a good idea, I'll try that too. I'm not sure I understood your last point, could you explain that again? Thanks :wink:

Thanks in advance.

Great question, <@UAL7940NM>. There are definitely scenarios I think about, though it's not a small set but is ever-growing. My ongoing struggle is usually to reduce the set to the more general, broader problems. I'm approaching this from the POV that almost all concepts in computed are 'constructions/inventions' and if we start by rejecting them all, what is the set of concepts that we could introduce that would not lead to the kinds of problems we currently have. (Rejecting is easy but forgetting is hard). I think attacking the 'problem' at all levels is useful.

Maybe we can all benefit from sharing our scenarios and derive principles from a more diverse set of use cases. What do you think?

attachments.fallback:
[May 30th, 2018 12:05 PM] stefanlesser: I was thinking recently that it would be great to have a list of values, sort of a manifesto, not unlike the Agile Manifesto, to at least have a discussion about the values we share, and to find out where we differ. I think a lot of research is opportunistic (and that's not a bad thing), but lacks a big picture of where we want to take this.

Ultimately, it should be something that allows developers of different platforms and languages to unite and at least discover shared values. One other thing we're incredibly good at is building all these silos of different language and platform communities, where we can all re-invent wheels all the time. I think there is a benefit in this language diversity, but it's hard to reap, because these different communities seldom talk to each other.

Thanks for linking back to this, <@U8A5MS6R1>! It’s good to see that this Slack is getting a little busier, even if that means it’ll take me a while to catch up.

Consider the manifesto a very early draft for discussion (I think it says that at the top, but just to be safe). It should obviously be a collection of *our* values. I just thought it would be good to get started with something we can iterate on.
